topic: Aberration refractive model (alpha = 2.56e-8, 20.5 arcsec) — Refuted by Data
change_summary: c3 holes H1 (6-29 deg), H2 (annual periodicity), H3 (paraphrase) all addressed; c3 H4 (anchor) also addressed by changing href to #section-1-8 (anchor verified to exist in part1.html). Net: detail_evidence grew 17.7% (2177->2562 chars) due to new annual-periodicity sentence; detail_verdict_text shrank 2.4% (1091->1065 chars) due to paraphrase fix.
our_argument_summary: Stellar aberration is achromatic; refraction is chromatic. Kramers-Kronig forbids any causal medium with n>1 from being achromatic. VLBI (~GHz) and optical/Gaia (~500 THz) measure the same aberration constant across six orders of magnitude in frequency, empirically falsifying any refractive mechanism. Annual periodicity additionally rules out any static refractive index. Dome alpha = 2.56e-8 has no derivation from dome geometry — value reverse-engineered from known kappa = v/c ~= 9.93e-5 rad ~= 20.49 arcsec. Section 1.8 cascade: any n(r) strong enough to displace the apparent sun must displace stars by orders of magnitude more than Gaia DR3 microarcsecond precision tolerates. Verdict: Refuted by Data with Part 3.1b structural post-hoc tag piled on.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-016 is a static one-line entry in raw-text/02-wins.txt:98: 'Annual Aberration Refractive Model CONFIRMED (alpha = 2.56e-8, 20.5 arcsec)'. There is no derivation, no formula, no live monitoring evident in the mirrored raw-text. The evolution log says the model appeared in v48.0; no source file connects alpha to disc_radius, firmament_height, sun_altitude, or any other dome parameter. Searches across all 8 raw-text files return zero non-trivial mentions of alpha or 2.56 outside the WIN registry line itself.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely declarative. The pair (alpha, 20.5 arcsec) is asserted as confirmed without any monitor.py domain, API call, or comparator routine in the mirrored raw-text. Verification = restating the textbook value. Note: the dome's actual monitor.py (in the dome's GitHub repo) is not mirrored in raw-text/; this review's tag inference is from the WIN-registry-only treatment evident in raw-text. If monitor.py contains hardcoded values for WIN-016 the 'hardcoded' tag is right; if it has nothing for WIN-016 the more accurate tag is 'none'. The c2/c3 reviewers chose 'hardcoded'; I keep that with a flag.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None — no test exists to fail.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None new. The boilerplate verdict-text phrase 'the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call' is structurally inaccurate for WIN-016 because there is no live fetch call associated with this WIN — see Hole #1 below.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-016 appears only as a static string in raw-text/02-wins.txt:98. No monitor.py domain or fetch call references the WIN in the mirrored raw-text; no live data is compared. Tag retained from c2/c3 (carries a small 'could be none if monitor.py has no entry at all' caveat) — keeping consistent with prior cycles since no new positive evidence flips it.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Stellar aberration has been textbook physics since Bradley (1728); the IAU 2000 constant is 20.49552 arcsec at J2000. The dome reproduces 20.5 arcsec but renames the cause from Earth's orbital velocity (v/c) to aetheric refractive perturbation. Same number, new label.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Aberration constant published 1728 and refined continuously; raw-text/06-evolution.txt:31 shows the WIN entered the dome model in v48.0, ~298 years after the original observation. The dome statement is value adoption, not a forecast.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: v/c = 29.7827/299792.458 = 9.93e-5 rad = 20.49 arcsec, matching the dome 20.5 arcsec to four sig figs. No expression connecting disc_radius=20015, firmament_height=9086, sun_altitude=5733, or any aetheric parameter to alpha=2.56e-8 or 20.5 arcsec is given anywhere in raw-text/. alpha = 2.56e-8 = 2.59 * (v/c)^2 — close to but not exactly (v/c)^2 (which would be 9.87e-9, a factor of 2.6 smaller). I tried disc_radius/c, firmament_height/c, sun_altitude/c, (sun_altitude/disc_radius)^2 and ^4 — none reproduce 2.56e-8. The numerical mismatch with simple dome combinations suggests reverse-engineering with a different velocity scale or pure parameter tuning, but no derivation is documented.

holes_found.description: Boilerplate-misapplied verdict text. detail_verdict_text reads 'Under the dome author's own definition ("Timestamped before confirming data"), the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call, making even that weaker test unverifiable.' But WIN-016 has NO live fetch call — the WIN appears only as a static one-line entry in the dome's WIN registry; there is no monitoring code, no API call, and no comparator function for it (per its own tag 'monitoring: hardcoded' / arguably 'none'). Saying the hardcoded value 'sits in the same commit as the live fetch call' implies a fetch exists and is co-committed; that's a strictly stronger claim than the truth, and a defender can correctly rebut 'there is no live fetch call here.' Cross-WIN check: the same boilerplate sentence appears in detail_verdict_text of WIN-016, 017, 018, 019, 029 (tagged hardcoded) and WIN-021, 047, 055 (tagged 'none'). Zero of these eight WINs has a live fetch. The boilerplate is wrong across all of them, but for WIN-016 specifically it weakens our otherwise-strong post-hoc structural argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the offending sentence in WIN-016 detail_verdict_text with: 'There is no live fetch call here at all — WIN-016 appears only as a one-line static entry in the dome WIN registry, with no monitor.py domain, no API source, and no comparator function. The "prediction" reduces to the bare statement that a textbook value matches the textbook value, with no apparatus that could ever produce a contrary observation.' Once landed for WIN-016, propose a parallel patch sweep for the other seven WINs with the same boilerplate (016, 017, 018, 019, 021, 029, 047, 055) — but those are out of scope for this review. Worth opening as a separate cross-WIN issue.

holes_found.description: Overgeneralized concession citation. detail_evidence contains: 'The dome's own AI context page lists the EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation as an open question, conceding that derived parameters lack theoretical basis.' Verified the source: raw-text/07-ai-context.txt:73 lists 'EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation' as item 6 of a V12/V13 task list. That is one specific coordinate-system parameter on a TODO list, NOT a sweeping admission about all derived parameters. Generalizing from one task-list item to 'derived parameters lack theoretical basis' overreads the concession. A capable defender could correctly counter: the dome admits one parameter has open physical interpretation; it does not concede that alpha=2.56e-8 (a different parameter on a different page entirely) lacks derivation. Our argument that alpha specifically lacks derivation stands on its own evidence (we already noted no formula exists in raw-text); we should not lean on a misapplied concession to reinforce it.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) narrow the framing: 'The dome's own AI context page lists "EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation" as an open task-list item, demonstrating that ad-hoc parameter fitting without derivation is acceptable practice in the dome model — but no parallel acknowledgment exists for alpha=2.56e-8 specifically'; or (b) drop the citation entirely and let the absence-of-derivation argument stand on its own. (a) is preferable — it preserves the rhetorical force without overstating the dome's concession.

holes_found.description: Bradley 1728 chromatic-attribution. Detail_evidence opens 'James Bradley (1728) explicitly tested and rejected atmospheric refraction as an explanation: refraction is wavelength-dependent (chromatic), while stellar aberration is achromatic.' Bradley's original 1728 paper rejected refraction primarily on the geometric ground that the apparent stellar displacement was perpendicular to the line-of-sight to the Sun, not radial along the line-of-sight as a refraction model would predict. The chromaticity argument is correct and well-known but is essentially a modern reformulation; attributing it to Bradley specifically is historically imprecise. The same imprecision propagates into tldr_evidence ('Bradley proved this distinction in 1728') and tldr_verdict ('a distinction proven in 1728'). A determined dome defender or historian could call this out. The argument itself (chromatic vs achromatic) remains valid; only the attribution is off.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to 'Refraction is wavelength-dependent (chromatic) while stellar aberration is achromatic across the entire electromagnetic spectrum — Bradley's 1728 paper introduced the broader case against atmospheric explanations on geometric grounds, and modern dispersion theory closes the case definitively.' Or simpler: drop the 'Bradley (1728) explicitly tested and rejected...' framing and instead say 'Stellar aberration has been distinguished from refraction since Bradley (1728); modern dispersion theory and direct VLBI-vs-optical tests close the case.' This preserves the historical anchor without the chromatic-attribution risk. (Curmudgeon TLDR guidance allows simplification; this isn't a TLDR, so precision matters.)

holes_found.description: c3 stronger_argument #1 (WIN-017 Gaia rhetorical handcuff) and #3 (steelmanned Kramers-Kronig response in detail_extra) remain unintegrated. detail_extra is still null. These were stronger_arguments not holes in c3, and the decider is not obligated to act on them — but they remain available wins. The advocate-mode defense_survives in c3 was 3 (the Kramers-Kronig steelman is the survivor); integrating the preemptive sentence would knock that to 1 per the c3 advocate_mode analysis.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional polish: add a detail_extra block with (a) the Gaia-handcuff sentence: 'The same Gaia DR3 catalog that anchors aberration to Earth's orbital velocity here also anchors stellar parallax to distance in WIN-017 — any escape that disqualifies Gaia for one falsifier breaks the other.' (b) the K-K steelman closeout: 'Even taken as a phenomenological deflection coefficient with no claim about an underlying refractive medium, alpha has no derivation from dome geometry, no mechanism for the precise one-year periodicity, and no explanation for why the same coefficient holds across radio and optical frequencies.' These are stronger-argument upgrades, not error fixes. Don't block on them.

stronger_arguments.description: Cross-WIN propagation issue from Hole #1 deserves its own ticket. The 'sits in the same commit as the live fetch call' boilerplate is wrong on 8 WINs (016, 017, 018, 019, 021, 029, 047, 055). Patching only WIN-016 fixes the local hole but leaves 7 others with the same misstatement. Worth surfacing this to the decider as a thesis-coherence issue: the post-hoc structural rider needs separate boilerplate for 'hardcoded but no fetch' WINs and 'no monitoring at all' WINs, distinct from the 'fetch-paired' template that presumably motivated the original wording.
sources: data/wins.json grep on phrase 'sits in the same commit as the live fetch call' — 8 hits, 0 of which actually have a live fetch

stronger_arguments.description: v/c=20.49 arcsec to four sig figs is the strongest single evidence the dome reverse-engineered alpha. Worth promoting from a bare statement to an explicit numeric pin: 'The dome's 20.5 arcsec value matches v/c=29.7827/299792.458=9.934e-5 rad=20.491 arcsec to one part in 4,000 — a precision the dome cannot derive from any combination of dome geometric parameters; the dome adopted the standard-physics number and labeled it confirmed.' This makes the post-hoc charge concrete and falsifiable — if the dome ever publishes a derivation of 20.5 arcsec from disc_radius/firmament_height, our claim is testable.
sources: v=29.7827 km/s (Earth orbital), c=299792.458 km/s (defined), arcsec/rad = 206264.806

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome correctly identifies stellar aberration as a real, well-measured ~20.5 arcsec phenomenon, and the cited value (20.5 arcsec) matches v/c to four significant figures. WIN-016's claim that the dome 'accommodates' this observation is true in the trivial sense — any model that adopts the standard-physics number can mechanically reproduce it. (See WIN-016.detail_claim 'Refractive index alpha = 2.56e-8 reproduces 20.5 arcsecond annual aberration' — accommodation, not derivation.)
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Accommodation is not prediction. The dome adds (a) a renamed cause (orbital velocity → aetheric refractive perturbation), (b) a free parameter alpha=2.56e-8 with no derivation from disc_radius=20015 km / firmament_height=9086 km / sun_altitude=5733 km (this review's derives_from_dome_evidence: zero non-trivial mentions of alpha in raw-text/), and (c) an implicitly static refractive medium incompatible with the annual periodicity (this review's H2 carryover and now-integrated detail_evidence sentence). Two of the three contributions are positively falsified — by Kramers-Kronig theoretically (raw-text/02-wins.txt:98 dome cite vs Bradley 1728 + dispersion theory), by VLBI/optical six-orders-of-magnitude empirical agreement (data/wins.json:WIN-016.detail_evidence), and by the annual-periodicity geometry that reduces to 'Earth orbits' under any other name. The verdict 'Refuted by Data' continues to hold.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 4 review of WIN-016 confirms verdict (Refuted by Data) holds firmly and that all four c3-flagged holes have been addressed in the patched content:

1. (H1, moderate) '6-29 deg' replaced with 'several degrees' / 'multiple degrees' — soft-phrasing route taken, not the derivation footnote. Acceptable: the soft phrasing aligns with what Section 1.8 actually substantiates and removes the unsourced upper bound. Trade-off: the steelmanned 29-deg cascade was the stronger version, but its absence doesn't undermine the argument.

2. (H2, moderate) Annual-periodicity sentence integrated almost verbatim from c3 recommendation: 'Independently, stellar aberration varies sinusoidally with a precise one-year period. A static refractive index cannot produce a time-varying angular displacement; the dome must either declare alpha time-varying with an annual phase (which is geometrically equivalent to reintroducing Earth's orbital motion under another name) or abandon the refractive interpretation entirely.' Closes a hole that drifted across two cycles.

3. (H3, minor) Paraphrase fixed — 'Timestamped before confirming data' now matches raw-text/03-predictions.txt:13 verbatim. Quotation marks are now legitimate.

4. (H4, minor) Section 1.8 anchor changed from #part1 to #section-1-8. Anchor verified to exist in data/sections.json:part1.html (line containing 'details id="section-1-8">'). Different from c3's recommended #p1-18 (which doesn't exist) but functionally correct.

New findings this cycle:

- Hole #1 (moderate): the verdict-text boilerplate 'sits in the same commit as the live fetch call' is structurally inaccurate for WIN-016, which has no live fetch call. This is also a cross-WIN issue (8 WINs share the wrong boilerplate). Strongly recommend per-WIN fix here and a separate cross-WIN sweep ticket.

- Hole #2 (minor): the EW-scale-0.9941 citation overgeneralizes from one V12/V13 task-list item to 'derived parameters lack theoretical basis.' Overstates the dome's concession.

- Hole #3 (minor): Bradley 1728 chromatic-attribution is historically imprecise — Bradley's original argument was geometric (perpendicular displacement), not chromatic. The chromaticity argument is valid; only the attribution is shaky.

- Hole #4 (minor, carryover): c3 stronger_arguments not integrated; detail_extra still null. Optional polish, not a real hole.

Quantitative re-verification: v/c = 9.934e-5 rad = 20.491 arcsec, matches dome 20.5 arcsec to 4 sig figs. alpha=2.56e-8 ~= 2.59*(v/c)^2 — no clean dimensionless combination of dome geometric parameters reproduces 2.56e-8. derives_from_dome=false confirmed.

Cross-WIN consistency: WIN-016, WIN-017 (Gaia parallax refutation), WIN-065 (Polaris excess) all rely on the same Gaia DR3 dataset and microarcsecond astrometry regime; any escape that disqualifies Gaia breaks all three. WIN-057 (curve-fitted equatorial ring) shares the same critique pattern as WIN-016's reverse-engineered alpha. No internal inconsistencies.

Net: verdict robust, three real new findings (one moderate, two minor), one carryover stronger-argument backlog. Recommend minor_edit on Hole #1 (boilerplate fix) and Hole #2 (EW-scale citation narrowing); Hole #3 is optional historical polish.

citations_verified: Gaia link https://www.cosmos.esa.int/web/gaia — ESA Gaia mission page, resolves
dome predictions page link https://john09289.github.io/predictions — verbatim 'Timestamped before confirming data' confirmed at raw-text/03-predictions.txt:13
Part 3.1b posthoc-standard anchor referenced; assumed to exist from prior c3 verification (not re-verified this cycle since no patches touched it)
v/c = 20.491 arcsec computed from Earth orbital velocity 29.7827 km/s and c=299792.458 km/s — matches dome 20.5 arcsec to 4 sig figs
EW scale 0.9941 in dome AI context as task-list item #6 — verified at raw-text/07-ai-context.txt:73; framing in WIN overgeneralizes (Hole #2)
1.8 billion stars Gaia DR3 figure — well-established Gaia DR3 catalog size
citations_unchecked: IAU 2000 aberration constant 20.49552 arcsec — not directly cited in WIN-016 prose but used as standard-value background
Bradley 1728 — historically established; chromatic-attribution is historically imprecise (Hole #3) but the citation itself is real

advocate_mode.best_defense: A capable dome defender steelmanning the patched WIN-016 against this c4 review would attack on three fronts: (1) The 'live fetch call' boilerplate is internally inconsistent — the reviewer admits there is no fetch yet writes verdict text claiming a co-commit problem with a fetch that doesn't exist; either the verdict text is right and our 'no fetch' claim is wrong, or the text is wrong and our broader attack on dome verification machinery is overplayed. (2) The EW-scale-0.9941 citation generalizes to 'derived parameters lack theoretical basis' — the dome only acknowledged one specific parameter on a task list, not a sweeping admission. The reviewer is using a single TODO item to indict all dome parameter-fitting practice. (3) The annual-periodicity argument is stronger if the medium is strictly static, but the dome has never claimed alpha is time-invariant; v48.0 evolution notes mention 'aetheric perturbation' which could be read as time-varying. The reviewer's 'static medium' interpretation is the strongest reading against the dome but is not anchored to any dome text saying 'alpha is constant in time.' (4) The Bradley 1728 chromatic-attribution is historically wrong (Bradley's argument was geometric); a defender could call this out as 'sloppy historical scholarship' and use it to undermine the review's apparent rigor.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Two text fixes preempt fronts (1) and (2). For (1) — fix the live-fetch-call boilerplate (Hole #1 in this review) — this hardens the post-hoc structural rider and removes the inconsistency. For (2) — narrow the EW-scale citation (Hole #2) — preserves rhetorical force without overreach. Front (3) is partially addressed by the integrated annual-periodicity sentence, which already names the geometric-equivalence escape; if anything, add one more sentence: 'No published dome derivation makes alpha time-varying; the v48.0 evolution log describes alpha as a fixed coefficient.' Front (4) is the historical-attribution polish (Hole #3), optional. With (1) and (2) landed, defense_survives drops from 2 to 1; with all four landed, drops to 0/5.

cross_win_consistency.notes: Scientific consistency: WIN-016 + WIN-017 (parallax-as-wobble Refuted by Data via Gaia DR3) + WIN-065 (Polaris excess Refuted by Data via stellar position data) all rely on Gaia DR3 microarcsecond astrometry. A defender who attacks Gaia to escape one breaks the other two. WIN-057 (Misleading — curve-fitted equatorial ring 14,105 km) shares the same reverse-engineered-parameter critique pattern as WIN-016's alpha=2.56e-8; no contradiction. Boilerplate consistency (newly noted this cycle): WIN-016, 017, 018, 019, 021, 029, 047, 055 all use the same 'sits in the same commit as the live fetch call' phrase in detail_verdict_text, but none of these eight WINs has a live fetch call (5 are 'monitoring: hardcoded', 3 are 'monitoring: none'). The boilerplate is wrong on all eight. Patching WIN-016 alone closes the local hole; the other seven need a parallel sweep — out of scope for this review but worth a cross-WIN ticket. No verdict-level inconsistencies; only a propagation-of-template issue.

claims_checked: v/c = 20.5 arcsec (dome claim and our acceptance)
alpha = 2.56e-8 has no derivation from dome geometry (review claim)
Stellar aberration varies sinusoidally with one-year period (newly added sentence)
Six orders of magnitude in frequency (radio ~GHz to optical ~500 THz)
quantitative_verification.computations: v/c = 29.7827/299792.458 = 9.9344e-5 rad. Convert to arcsec: 9.9344e-5 * 206264.806 = 20.491 arcsec. Matches dome 20.5 arcsec to 1 part in ~4,000 (better than 4 sig figs). (v/c)^2 = 9.869e-9; alpha/(v/c)^2 = 2.56e-8 / 9.869e-9 = 2.595 — close to but not exactly (v/c)^2; suggests reverse engineering with a different velocity scale or unrelated tuning. Tested geometric combinations: disc_radius/c=6.68e-5, firmament_height/c=3.03e-5, sun_altitude/c=1.91e-5, (sun_altitude/disc_radius)^2=0.082, (sun_altitude/disc_radius)^4=0.0067 — none reproduce 2.56e-8. No simple dimensionless dome-parameter combination matches alpha. Frequency span: VLBI typically operates in 1-100 GHz; Gaia/optical at ~500 THz. 5e14 / 1e9 = 5e5, log10(5e5) = 5.7 — closer to 'six orders of magnitude' than to seven. Claim 'six orders of magnitude' is correct (rounded from 5.7 toward six in shorthand). Annual periodicity: aberration vector traces a sinusoid with period 365.25 days, amplitude 20.5 arcsec, phase set by orbital position; this is observationally established and not in dispute.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: None new this cycle. The c3 6-29 deg discrepancy was resolved by replacing with 'several degrees' / 'multiple degrees'.
